Party_Details.Name: Dynasty Line Ltd (in liquidation)
Party_Details.Law_Firm: Rodyk & Davidson LLP/ Premier Law LLC

Facts.Text: Dynasty Line Ltd was incorporated under the laws of the BVI
Sia Sukamto and Lee Howe Yong were appointed directors of Dynasty
Dynasty acquired 29,537,367 shares in China Development Corporation Limited for HK$230,391,463
Vendors transferred shares to Dynasty before the completion date
Dynasty charged 60,161,510 CDC shares to Commerzbank as security for facilities granted to Sia
Dynasty charged 28 million CDC shares to Societe Generale as security for facilities granted to Beswil Investment Pte Ltd
Dynasty charged 48,822,700 CDC shares to KG Investments Asia Limited as security for facilities granted to Franklin Syah
Dynasty charged 10,702,625 CDC shares to Creditanstalt Bankverein as security for facilities granted to Sia

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the directors of a company can be held legally responsible for actions that contravene their management obligations when the company is insolvent or near insolvency
Party_Details.Rule: Directors must act in the best interests of the company and its creditors when the company is insolvent or on the verge of insolvency
Party_Details.Application: The directors breached their fiduciary duties and failed to act in good faith by pledging company assets for personal benefit when the company was insolvent or on the verge of insolvency.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that the pledging of shares was not improper and was done in the interests of Dynasty. The directors acted honestly and in good faith, and the company was not insolvent at the material time.

Party_Details.Name: Sia Sukamto

Facts.Text: Sia Sukamto was appointed director of Dynasty Line Ltd
Sia acquired shares in CDC through Dynasty for HK$230,391,463
Sia arranged for Dynasty to charge CDC shares to Commerzbank as security for his facilities
Sia arranged for Dynasty to charge CDC shares to Societe Generale for facilities granted to Beswil Investment Pte Ltd
Sia arranged for Dynasty to charge CDC shares to KG Investments Asia Limited for facilities granted to Franklin Syah
Sia arranged for Dynasty to charge CDC shares to Creditanstalt Bankverein for his facilities

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director can be held legally responsible for pledging company assets when the company is not insolvent
Party_Details.Rule: Directors are required to act in the best interests of the company and its creditors when the company is insolvent or on the verge of insolvency
Party_Details.Application: The company was solvent at the time of the transactions, and the pledging of shares was executed in good faith and squarely within the directors' rights.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that the company was not insolvent at the time of the transactions and the directors acted within their rights.

Party_Details.Name: Lee Howe Yong

Facts.Text: Lee Howe Yong was appointed director of Dynasty Line Ltd
Lee was promised 20% of Dynasty's profits without any equity contribution
Dynasty charged 28 million CDC shares to Societe Generale as security for facilities granted to Beswil Investment Pte Ltd, a company owned by Sia and Lee

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director can be held legally responsible for actions taken in the interest of the company when the company is not insolvent
Party_Details.Rule: Directors must act in the best interests of the company and its creditors when the company is insolvent or on the verge of insolvency
Party_Details.Application: The company was solvent at the material times, and the actions taken were executed honestly and in the best commercial interests of the company.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that the company was not insolvent at the time of the transactions and the directors acted within their rights.

Party_Details.Name: Sia Sukamto

Facts.Text: Sia and Low settled Suit 960 on 1998-11-05, and Low agreed to discontinue the action

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the defendant by counterclaim acted in breach of the settlement agreement and engaged in a conspiracy to injure
Party_Details.Rule: A party to a settlement agreement must not pursue related claims if it contravenes the terms of the agreement
Party_Details.Application: Low contravened the personal settlement agreement and engaged in an unlawful conspiracy to injure by procuring the liquidators to commence claims against Sia through the company.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that the claims pursued by Dynasty were separate from the settlement agreement and did not constitute a breach or an unlawful conspiracy.

Party_Details.Name: Low Tuck Kwong

Facts.Text: Low presented a petition in the Hong Kong High Court for the appointment of provisional liquidators for Dynasty.
Provisional Liquidators commenced proceedings against Sia and Lee in HCA 2057 of 2007.
Low filed a petition in the BVI High Court to wind up Dynasty.
Low's application to wind up Dynasty was granted and liquidators were appointed.
The Liquidators commenced the Original Action against Sia and Lee.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the defendants by counterclaim breached the settlement agreement and engaged in a conspiracy to injure
Party_Details.Rule: A party to a settlement agreement must not pursue related claims if it contravenes the terms of the agreement
Party_Details.Application: The claims pursued by Dynasty were independent corporate actions brought by the liquidators that did not contravene the personal settlement agreement, and there was no conspiracy to injure.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that the claims pursued by Dynasty were separate from the settlement agreement and did not constitute a breach or an unlawful conspiracy.

Party_Details.Name: Lau Wu Kwai King Lauren

Facts.Text: The individual was a director of Stone Forest Corporate Advisory and was initially engaged by the primary creditor to investigate the company's affairs prior to any formal appointment.
Following her appointment as a provisional liquidator, she entered into a funding agreement with the primary creditor to finance investigations and subsequent legal actions against the directors.
She authorized the commencement of the Original Action based on her internal findings of misfeasance, which the Plaintiff alleged were 'predetermined' and 'unbalanced'.
The individual refused to instruct solicitors to accept service of the counterclaim in Singapore, compelling the Plaintiff to incur costs for service out of jurisdiction.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a liquidator's reliance on creditor funding and close coordination with a creditor during an investigation constitutes an unlawful conspiracy to injure the target of that investigation.
Party_Details.Rule: A liquidator's pursuit of a lawsuit is protected if their predominant purpose is asset recovery for the company, even if the action is funded by a hostile creditor and results in injury to a director.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that the individual acted as an independent officer. While she was funded by Low, her investigation was based on legitimate evidence of share pledging. Her refusal to facilitate service of the counterclaim was 'unhelpful' but did not prove a predominant intent to injure.

Party_Details.Name: William Tacon

Facts.Text: The individual was appointed as a joint liquidator in the BVI and relied entirely on the investigative reports prepared by his co-liquidator (Lauren).
During cross-examination, the individual admitted he had not conducted his own detailed enquiries or independent verification of the claims before authorizing the lawsuit.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a joint liquidator who acts as a 'rubber stamp' for another liquidator's investigation can be held liable for conspiracy to injure.
Party_Details.Rule: A lack of diligence or passive reliance on a co-liquidator does not equate to the specific intent required for the tort of conspiracy.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court categorized the individual as a 'sleeping' liquidator. While his lack of independent enquiry was noted, it did not satisfy the high threshold of proving he combined with others with the predominant purpose of injuring the director.

Party_Details.Name: Dynasty Line Ltd (in liquidation)

Facts.Text: The company was placed into liquidation following a petition by the primary creditor and was used as the nominal plaintiff to recover US$66m in assets.
The company's liquidators pursued the action despite a personal settlement agreement between the creditor and the director.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a company can be liable for conspiracy if it is alleged to be the 'alter ego' of a creditor who is using the liquidation process to bypass a settlement agreement.
Party_Details.Rule: A company in liquidation remains a separate legal entity; its pursuit of claims for the benefit of the estate is a legitimate corporate act and not an act of conspiracy.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court rejected the 'alter ego' argument. The company was the proper plaintiff for claims of breach of fiduciary duty, and its pursuit of those claims—authorized by court-appointed liquidators—could not constitute an unlawful conspiracy.
